name: Danielle Sered
practice: Restorative justice, violence intervention, survivor-centered accountability
approach: Incarceration does not make survivors safer. Common Justice runs an alternative-to-incarceration program where the person who caused harm meets with the person they harmed. The survivor defines what accountability looks like. Most survivors choose this over prison.
body_of_work: Founded Common Justice — the first alternative-to-incarceration program for violent felonies in the US. Until We Reckon (2019). Shows that when you ask survivors of violence what they want, most don't want prison — they want safety, accountability, and answers.
why_instructive: She proves that the dome's legal layer doesn't have to mean incarceration. Accountability without cages. Safety without surveillance. The dome must offer this path.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — alternative to incarceration for violent felonies
dome_relevance.4: Health — trauma healing for both survivors and those who caused harm
dome_relevance.8: Community — restorative process rebuilds community trust

sphere_relevance.5: Social — community-based accountability changes neighborhood dynamics
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — proves viable alternatives to incarceration at scale
